Bitrefill, one of the largest crypto e-commerce platforms, just disclosed a cyberattack that took place on March 1.  The entry point was a compromised employee laptop.   From there, attackers exfiltrated a legacy credential, used it to access production secrets, transferred funds from hot wallets, and exposed 18,500 purchase records containing email addresses, payment addresses, and IP addresses.  Bitrefill hasn't disclosed the total financial impact but says it will absorb the losses from operational capital.  The company's investigation found strong similarities with past operations by North Korea's Lazarus Group, also known as Bluenoroff, based on malware signatures, on-chain tracing, and reused attacker infrastructure.  Credit to Bitrefill for the transparency with a detailed incident report, direct user notifications, and immediate steps to tighten access controls and monitoring.   That accountability matters, but the attack pattern is familiar.   Bybit's $1.5 billion loss last year also started with a compromised laptop. The attack surface keeps shifting from protocols to people and the operational layers around them. 🚨 Exposition d'un serveur APT28 (FANCY BEAR) : une fuite historique pour le GRU Russe  En janvier 2026, des chercheurs en threat intelligence (Hunt.io puis Ctrl-Alt-Intel) ont découvert un open directory sur un serveur de command-and-control (C2) d’APT28 (alias Fancy Bear / Sednit / Forest Blizzard / GRU Unit 26165).  Les faits clés - IP exposée: 203.161.50.145   - Hébergé sur un VPS Namecheap (Phoenix, États-Unis)   - Port : 8889 (SimpleHTTPServer Python)   - Découvert le 13 janvier 2026 - Ce même serveur avait déjà été publiquement attribué à APT28 par le CERT-UA en octobre 2024! Le groupe l’utilisait encore malgré la découverte. Erreur OpSec monumentale.  Ce qui a été exposé (61 fichiers, 52 Mo, ça parait peu mais pourtant…) - 2 800 e-mails exfiltrés (gouvernements, militaires, diplomates: focus Ukraine + Balkans/NATO)   - 240 jeux de credentials complets (login + mot de passe + secrets TOTP pour contourner la 2FA)   - 11 500 contactes issus des carnets d’adresses des victimes   - 140 règles de forwarding silencieuses créées dans les boîtes mail (redirection invisible vers les attaquants)   - Payloads XSS avancés (injection CSS, vol de credentials Chrome, extraction TOTP Roundcube)   - Outils complets : serveur Flask, implants Go, historique bash des opérateurs, payloads Roundcube/SquirrelMail/Zimbra/MDaemon   - Preuves d’exploitation de CVE-2023-43770 (Roundcube)  Campagnes révélées - Operation Roundish (Hunt.io : 11 mars 2026)   - Operation RoundPress (ESET)   - Chaînes de phishing ClickFix + faux reCAPTCHA déjà signalées par CERT-UA    Ciblage prioritaire : forces aériennes roumaines, ministères de la Défense, think tanks, énergie, télécoms, santé en Europe de l’Est.  Sources principales - Hunt.io « Operation Roundish » (11 mars 2026)   - Ctrl-Alt-Intel « FancyBear Exposed » (mi-mars 2026)   - CERT-UA (attribution 2024 confirmée)  C’est rare de voir les « entrailles » d’une opération GRU aussi clairement exposées. Un cadeau pour la threat intelligence mondiale… et un rappel brutal que même les services russes font des fautes basiques d’OpSec.

Most people have never heard about Bitcoin’s biggest bug… On August 15, 2010, a vulnerability allowed someone to create 184,467,440,737 BTC in a single transaction. That’s over 8,000× the total supply cap of 21 million. The issue? An integer overflow bug that broke Bitcoin’s supply rules inside block #74638. The network didn’t catch it immediately, but within hours, developers (including Satoshi) identified the exploit.  The fix: Bug patched Network rolled back First-ever emergency hard fork executed The invalid coins were erased. Permanently. Since then?  Nothing like it has ever happened again. Bitcoin didn’t just survive its biggest failure… it proved it could defend itself. #Bitcoin #BTC #CryptoHistory #Blockchain #Satoshi #Web3

This thread is still under 1% of the larger dossier I built over a year across 2 Android devices and 2 iPhones.  @Apple @AppleSupport @SenatorHeinrich   Qualified DFIR teams, researchers, journalists, and incident responders who want raw logs can request them directly.  The public slice already shows enough to understand the pattern: persistent-store corruption, trust/auth churn, hidden workload forcing, cleanup violence, and system-shell faults.  What the last findings show is not random app instability. They show a tampering signature concentrated in Apple first-party state, trust, memory, and control layers.  The pattern that matters is this:  •persistent-store corruption  •auth / trust churn  •hidden background workload  •repeated memory-pressure cleanup  •system-shell guard faults  •repeated diagnostic captures  •cross-device continuity over a year  That is why this reads as surveillance-style tampering / C2-like operational pressure, not normal phone behavior.  The craziest findings and why they matter  1) coreduetd-2026-03-17-013310.ips  What hit:   Apple CoreDuet / people-suggestion / contact-linked state What happened: integrity-check and SQLite I/O abort path Why it matters: CoreDuet is not some random toy app. When a first-party stateful daemon aborts in abortAfterFailingIntegrityCheck / handleSqliteIOError, that points to poisoned or corrupted persistent state. In case you missed it: What happens when the FortiGate next-generation firewall protecting your network becomes the backdoor? 🚪  Throughout early 2026, our DFIR team has been tracking a wave of FortiGate NGFW compromises. We have observed attackers exploiting vulnerabilities to extract config files, steal service account credentials, and move laterally.   The worst part? Most organizations lack the log retention to see how it happened.   In a report from earlier this month, SentinelLABS and SentinelOne DFIR researchers break down the attacker playbook.   Could this get worse? Yes. LLMs are acting as a cheat code, boosting lower-skilled threat actors to conduct these exact types of attacks.  Because AI models are trained on these NGFW appliances, they give attackers a step-by-step guide on how to gain access and move deeper into your network. Expect attack volumes on exposed edge devices to keep rising.  Because you typically cannot install EDR on edge devices like FortiGate, your defense strategy has to shift. You need to stream your logs to a SIEM.  Get the full threat breakdown and defense playbook from SentinelLABS researcher Alex Delamotte and DFIR members Stephen Bromfield, Mary Braden Murphy, and Amey Patne: s1.ai/frtgate26

However, since the cyberattack began on March 14, the company's servers have been knocked offline, meaning even sober drivers can't start their vehicles if their device is due for calibration.  The outage is affecting drivers across 46 states, with roughly 150,000 people using the system nationwide. sample_tweets.text: 1. Not true. Minor exception.  This is a long-standing conspiracy dating back to the very inception of malware, in essence the idea governments and anti-malware vendors are cooperating with each other for espionage. The reality is this is simply improbable. Not every anti-malware vendor resides in the United States and not every anti-malware vendor has to cooperate with the United States government. Additionally, some anti-malware services may feel hostile toward the United States government and actively disregard any form of communication.  However, there have been some instances where the United States government has partnered with anti-malware vendors and/or security companies to target high-profile targets (sex traffickers, terrorist organizations) and requested assistance. It would not be outside the realm of possibility to intentionally insert an exception in highly targeted operations.  This has been semi-documented in the past whereas Google identified a malware campaign in the Middle East and it was discovered to be a United States military operation targeting individuals believed to be part of ISIS.  Finally, Magic Lantern is old. It is old as dirt. It was discussed in the early-2000's. Malware has changed a lot since then. The anti-malware industry has changed a lot. This sort of operation (wide spread espionage via malware) just isn't really possible without global cooperation, including China and Russia.  2. Not true. Long standing schizo theory. Google it. Even real privacy schizos know it's not true. The concern arose when security researchers identified a debug switch in INTEL ME. Additionally, if this were true, network traffic monitoring software would identify this.  There is also open source solutions, you don't need INTEL ME or anything else.  The exception to this is when the United States government intercepts hardware and places malware on it or intentionally modifies it. This is true.  3. Partially true. There is some speculation, but basically the NSA recommended Dual_EC_DRBG to vendors as a standard despite criticism of it and known vulnerabilities in which could allow exploitation. Basically, the NSA was recommending a known bad thing.  4. No idea. I don't do anything with frequencies and radios.  5. This is true.  6. This is true. However, this is not exclusive to the NSA.  7. Partially true. The United States government owns a bunch of Tor nodes and monitors it, the monitoring however is for entry and exit of Tor. However, this cannot easily identify you. If this were the case then there would be much less child pornography and fentanyl sales on Tor. Additionally, they would use this to heavily crackdown on ransomware groups.  Most of the time people are caught on Tor from information leaking from Tor (long story, basically cookies)  8. Partially true. It has been documented several times large tech organizations are aware of critical exploits and (based on existing contracts with them) may notify them before anyone else due to the risk to critical infrastructure of the United States. Microsoft has big contracts with the United States. This isn't a surprise. Furthermore, it was been speculated heavily that Microsoft has delayed patches to aid the United States military in offensive cyber operations (APT NightEagle)  9. This is true. However, to the extent they can "take it over" is ambiguous because your cars electronics and GPS are not connected to your steering wheel.  10. IoT is a huge piece of shit and is compromised all the time. Seriously, don't use IoT devices.  11. No idea.

🤖 [GEMINI 분석] 2026-03-22 17:31 KST
🟠 위협 점수: 8/10 (높음)

### 1. 현재 주요 사이버보안 트렌드
최근 공격자들은 **Supply Chain Attack**을 통해 Trivy와 같은 보안 도구 및 GitHub Actions를 악용하여 광범위한 인프라를 타겟팅하고 있습니다. 또한, **Callback Phishing** 기법이 고도화되어 Microsoft Azure Monitor와 같은 신뢰받는 클라우드 서비스 알림을 사칭하는 사례가 급증하고 있습니다. 더불어, **Agentic AI** 생태계의 취약점과 MCP(Model Context Protocol) 서버의 보안 가시성 부족이 새로운 Shadow IT 위협으로 부상하고 있습니다.

### 2. 주요 위협 행위자 / 캠페인
- **Larva-26002 (AhnLab ASEC):** MS-SQL 서버를 대상으로 스캐너를 설치하고 랜섬웨어(Trigona, Mimic) 유포를 시도함.
- **TeamPCP (BleepingComputer):** Trivy 스캐너 공급망 공격을 통해 GitHub Actions 환경에서 credential-stealing malware를 유포함.
- **Russian Intelligence Services (TheHackerNews):** WhatsApp 및 Signal 등 메신저 계정 탈취를 목적으로 한 대규모 피싱 캠페인 수행.
- **Void Manticore / Handala Hack (Check Point Research):** 이란 연계 해커 그룹으로, 데이터 삭제(Wiper) 공격 및 'hack and leak' 전술을 구사함.
- **CipherForce (AhnLab ASEC):** 국내 취업 포털을 타겟으로 한 신규 위협 행위자로 활동 중.

### 3. 한국 관련 위협
- **국내 APT 공격:** 안랩 보고서에 따르면 2026년 2월 국내 타겟 APT 공격이 지속적으로 모니터링되고 있으며, 특히 취업 포털을 겨냥한 공격이 확인됨.
- **MS-SQL 타겟팅:** 국내 운영 중인 MS-SQL 서버가 Larva-26002 공격자의 주요 타겟으로 식별됨.
- **기타:** 이스트시큐리티 ESRC는 저작권 경고를 위장한 PyChain 캠페인 및 가짜 FileZilla 사이트를 통한 악성코드 유포를 경고함.

### 4. 주요 CVE / 취약점
- **CVE-2026-21992:** Oracle Identity Manager 및 Web Services Manager, Unauthenticated RCE, **Critical**, 악용 가능성 높음.
- **CVE-2026-3888:** Ubuntu Desktop (Snap), Local Privilege Escalation, **High**, 로컬 권한 상승 위험.
- **CVE-2025-32975:** Quest KACE, 교육 분야 타겟으로 악용 정황 확인, **High**.
